rules.text: Don't speak of caring with the word "love"; show the trace it leaves.
Don't criticize directly; let the subject embarrass themselves.
Find a common target of criticism with the listener.
Use common criticism humorously, not preachy.
Keep expression low friction: easily understood beats too clever.
Use Urban Dictionary as a vocabulary quarry, but adapt words to your own mouth, rhythm, and environment.
Don't name the thing; name the evidence.
Don't name the act; name the trace it leaves behind.
Don't name the emotion; name the body signal.
Don't name the state; name the symptom.
Don't name the desire; name the setup around it.
Keep the target obvious, but don't say it out loud.
Use innuendo/hinting instead of naming directly.
A car is not a car: it is lane lines, dashboard lights, and the hum of the engine.
A kiss is not a kiss: it is breath, taste, lipstick, and silence.
Heat is not heat: it is sweat, salt, open windows, and dead fans.
A hangover is not a hangover: it is too bright sun, dry mouth, and lost shoes.
Desire is not desire: it is keys, doors, pauses, and bad ideas.
The listener must get the hint in a second.
Give the song one recurring object, phrase, place, or counter.
Don't just write a mood; write a chain of evidence.
Every verse must change the meaning of the recurring thing.
Use setup -> payoff structure: plant a small detail at the beginning and pay it off later in a new light.
Use rule of three structure: two expected things and a third that twists or reveals.
Use callback: bring the same word, object, or image back so it feels earned.
Build pressure: every verse must raise the stakes.
Use status: show who thinks they control the situation and who is actually leaking onto the table.
Build a false expectation and twist it with the last word or image.
Detail beats generality.
The character must want something right now, not just float in the mood.
The obstacle reveals the character: a person is seen by what they do when the card doesn't work, the door doesn't open, or the answer doesn't come.
Write a scene, not a statement.
End the verse or song with a punchy button line that lingers.
Give the scene its own weird rule and escalate it.
Use dramatic irony: the listener understands more than the singer admits.
Use subtext: the line says one thing and means another.
Make the situation a pressure cooker: last bus, 3% battery, taxi waiting, door half open.
One line can stand a disconnect, but the whole song must stand the night bus.
Give the character one ridiculously concrete principle they won't compromise on.
Make the character's name and mission the same thing.
Make the rebellion a low-stakes rebellion.
Give the character a happy "no".
The opponent must be an instantly recognizable power structure.
Make the character an underdog who doesn't perceive themselves as an underdog.
Give the character their own rule that the world tries to break.
Don't let the character win by crushing others; let them win by remaining the same.
Make the catchphrase an identity statement.
Use repetition so the second time is evidence.
Make the opponent accidentally comical.
Give the character escape, not talk.
The character must be cosplayable with one item.
Make the character positively obsessed.
Give the listener a reason to be on the character's side before the joke is explained.
Make the character's boundary childishly clear but emotionally big.
Use keeping their own name as the measure of victory.
Avoid typical AI words (e.g. 'neon', 'shadows', 'night's embrace', 'echo', 'destiny').
Do not write too neat or perfect rhymes if they feel forced.
Avoid artificial drama or grandiosity; prefer everyday realism.
Do not preach or moralize; let the listener draw their own conclusions.
Use colloquial language and dialects to break the sterility of standard language.
Do not use "3% battery left"
